content.full_text:
April 12, 1976

Roslyn Cooperman, Coordinator
Women For:
8913 West Olympic Boulevard
Beverly Hills, California 90211

Dear Ms. Cooperman:

     This letter is in response to your inquiry of April 7, 1976 concerning your calendar of events mailing.

     The Commission considered at its meeting last week a regulation redefining the term "expenditure" in light of the U.S. Supreme Court's decision in Buckley v. Valeo. Although the Commission has not yet adopted the regulation, it was the members' consensus that expenditures for communications which do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or measure are not reportable by groups such as WOMEN FOR. Therefore you do not have to observe the mass mailing requirements nor report the costs of printing and mailing the calendars.

     I will keep you informed of the Commission's actions regarding the applicability of disclosure requirements to WOMEN FOR:.

     If you have further questions, please contact our staff again.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Nan Hambleton
                                        Special Compliance Representative

NH/bw

women for:

continuing volunteer support and action for issues, candidates and legislation of our choice on local, state and national levels.

March 11, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Sirs:

On March 1, 1976, we sent you copies of our newsletter for March-
April 1976, indicating we had sent 1,405 pieces to our 1976 paid-
up members, with a handgun control petition enclosed, and 1,032
pieces to the 1975 members who have not yet renewed their member-
ships, without the petition enclosed. Because of the weight of
the petition, the mailing sent to our 1976 paid-up members cost
twice as much.

Our question relates to the reporting of financial contribution
to Proposition 15, the Nuclear Safeguards ballot measure. In
the four page newsletter five half-column lines were included
reading as follows: "Californians for Nuclear Safeguards, the
coalition of organizations sponsoring the Proposition 15 campaign,
needs help of all kinds. You can volunteer your time and/or
financial support. Write or call 6022 Wilshire Blvd., L.A. 90036
or 935-1223."

Will you please advise is how we estimate, and what we should
therefore list in our next report to you, the cost estimate of
these five half-column lines. Perhaps there's even a question
whether this item in our newsletter constitutes a reportable
financial contribution to Proposition 15.

Thank you very much for your prompt reply to this request for an
advisory opinion.

Sincerely,

ROSLYN COOPERMAN
Coordinator

Sent to 1875 and 1976 memb

HANDGUN CONTROL INITIATIVE TO HALT AMERICA'S GUN TRAGEDY


We are enclosing a petition for the Handgun Control Initiative for the November ballot, for you to gather signatures.

In addition to WOMEN FOR:, this initiative is supported by the Los Angeles Police Commission, Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors, Los Angeles Board of Education, and many local state and federal legislators and members of the Los Angeles City Council.

Here is your chance as WOMEN FOR: members to make a real impact on the safety of all our lives. We CAN stop the proliferation of hand guns in California (2½ million sold yearly in the United States). We CAN make our homes, schools and streets safer. The United States now has the highest homicide rate of any country. Handguns serve no purpose except to kill.


WOMEN FOR: HAS MADE A COMMITMENT TO GATHER SIGNATURES FOR THIS INITIATIVE - WE NEED YOUR HELP TO CIRCULATE PETITIONS!

In circulating the petition, please remember:

1. You must be a registered voter in the county in which you circulate the petition.

2. Those signing the petition must be registered to vote in the county in which they are signing.

3. Voters must sign the petition exactly as they are registered to vote. (Mr., Mrs., Miss, Ms. no longer necessary.)

4. Sign a petition yourself, and don't forget to sign all the petitions that you circulate, as the circulator, at the bottom.

5. Send petition with as many signatures as you can get to the Coalition for Handgun Control, 3455 Beverly Blvd., Los Angeles, 90048.

6. For information or more petitions, call the Handgun Control Coalition office, 653-1166.

PLEASE HELP!

WE NEED 500,000 SIGNATURES BY MAY 26th TO QUALIFY THIS INITIATIVE!

If you can give a few hours of your time to staff a table at Century City, or any other area, call the WOMEN FOR: office, 557-7411, to volunteer.

Sent to 1975 & 1976 members

womenfor:
NEWSLETTER

March-April, 1976

RAMSEY CLARK URGES ACTION TO DEFEAT S.1

Ramsey Clark, former U.S. Attorney General, and currently a Democratic candidate for the U.S. Senate in New York state, reviewed the legislative history of S.1, the so-called "Criminal Justice Reform Act of 1975," at the January 26 general membership meeting of WOMEN FOR: at the Beverly Hilton Hotel.

In 1966 a National Commission on Reform of Criminal Laws was appointed by President Johnson pursuant to a statute requiring it to be bipartisan. Former California Governor Pat Brown chaired the 12-member Commission, which submitted its final report on January 6, 1971, to President Richard Nixon and the Congress. Richard Nixon, at the height of his "law and order" campaign, called upon Attorney General John Mitchell and later, Richard Kleindienst, to rewrite the bipartisan commission's final report, and on March 14, 1973 he called upon Senators McClellan and Hruska to introduce the administration's "Criminal Code Reform Act of 1973." Louis B. Schwartz, professor of criminal law at the University of Pennsylvania, and director of the Brown Commission, characterized the Act as a "program of primitive vengefulness," which

contradicted in every respect the recommendations of the National Commission.

Senators McClellan and Hruska held subsequent hearings on the bill and on January 15, 1975, introduced it as S.1 - the "Criminal Code Reform Act of 1975" - with broad and powerful sponsorship.

S.1 extends government power over individuals in the form of wiretapping and other secret surveillance, giving broad discretion to officials in decisions about punishment, authorizing exceptionally severe sentences, restricting access to critical information about government operations and disregarding first amendment rights of free speech and assembly.

In the opinion of Ramsey Clark and many other legal authorities committed to the preservation of American constitutional rights, S.1 is inherently unamendable and should be recommitted for complete overhaul and redrafting.

Write today to your representative in Congress and to Senators Tunney and Cranston, urging the defeat of the disastrous legacy of the Nixon years, Senate Bill 1.

FROM THE COORDINATOR

Almost fifty years ago, Justice Brandeis stated: "Public discussion is a political duty." It is Women For's expectation that in this bicentennial election year, all of us will engage in the process of open dialogue that can best make our system work. Study-ing and debating issues should lead to action - and that is what we're all about.

It is time to move beyond the disillusioned disappointment of the post-Watergate period and once again share the feeling that what we say and do counts. Women For believes in the value of the person, and in the individual's ability to reach out and affect that amorphous entity called "the government."

We need to regenerate a personal excitement about the challenges and responsibilities that give life to our

democracy. Assemblyman John Vasconcellos is concerned with humanizing politics, shedding cynicism and revitalizing public participation. He is establishing a statewide link-up of people who concur in this positive belief in self-determination and humanistic social change. Some of his co-sponsors are Senator Anthony Beilenson, Congresswoman Yvonne Brathwaite Burke, Congresswoman George Leonard, Carl Rogers - and me. Write John Vasconcellos in Sacramento if you are interested in this philosophical/political network.

In the meantime, extend yourself with Women For. Join us to select and support candidates and issues. The next 200 years depend on our understanding that what we conscientiously dare to do today determines what style of life we will have tomorrow.

INITIATIVE MEASURE TO BE SUBMITTED DIRECTLY TO THE ELECTORS.

The Attorney General of California has prepared the following title and summary of the chief purpose and points of the proposed measure:

HANDGUN REGULATION: INITIATIVE STATUTE. Permits surrender of handguns prior to December 31, 1977; directs Legislature to provide compensation therefor. Effective January 1, 1978, prohibits purchase, transfer, or possession of operable handguns, and handgun ammunition, with prescribed exceptions, including possession by police, antique gun collectors, gun club members, security guards and gun dealers. Creates special fund from fines for handgun crimes to compensate for surrendered guns and for appropriations from fund to indemnify crime victims, after such compensation is used. Imposes state administered handgun licensing and registration requirements. Repeals local authority to license sale, carrying of handguns. Prescribes penalties for violations. Financial impact: Adoption of this measure will result in increased state and local one time costs of at least $3,783,000 which sum does not include amounts necessary to reimburse owners of surrendered handguns.

Upon a second conviction under like circumstances the additional period of imprisonment shall be for not less than 10 years nor for more than 15 years, and upon a third conviction for a like offense the additional period of imprisonment shall be for not less than 23 years, each term of additional imprisonment to run consecutively.

Upon a fourth or subsequent conviction under like circumstances the person so convicted may be imprisoned for life or for a term of not less than 23 years, within the discretion of the court in which such fourth or subsequent conviction was had. All terms of imprisonment imposed under this section shall run consecutively with any other term or sentence of a robbery, assault with a deadly weapon, murder, assault with intent to commit murder, rape, burglary, or kidnaping, upon conviction of such crime; in addition to the mandatory period thereof imposed. He prohibited in imprisonment in the state prison for a period of not less than five years from the expiration or termination of the sentence imposed for the crime of which he is convicted and shall not thereafter own or possess any firearm.

This section shall apply even in those cases where the use of a weapon is an element of the offense.

12023. In the instance of a person charged with committing or attempting to commit robbery, burglary, or any other crime mentioned in Section 12050, or while armed with any pistol, revolver, or other firearm capable of being concealed upon the person, without having a license or permit to carry the same, it shall be competent evidence of his intent to commit the felony if such person had been found face evidence of his intent to commit the felony in such region was used in the commission of the offense.

Article 23. Persons Authorized to Purchase, Possess, or Transfer Handguns

12025 (a) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, any person is guilty of a felony and is punishable by imprisonment in a county jail for not more than one year or, upon conviction of subsequent convictions, by imprisonment in a county jail for not more than two years if he commits, transfers, owns, or has in his possession or under his custody or control any handgun of the following categories:

(1) is an addicted incompetent,
(2) has been convicted of a crime involving the use or possession of any firearm or handgun or dangerous device, or has been acquitted by reason of insanity of any offense involving the use or possession of any firearm or handgun or dangerous device" means any explosive, incendiary or poison gas bomb, grenade, mine or similar device, black powder, or similar powder weapons or explosive to use a weapon.

(3) Any person who furnishes any handgun to any person listed in paragraph (a) and (a) is guilty of a felony and is punishable as prescribed in subdivision (a).

12023.1 Notwithstanding any other provision of law, any person who has been convicted of a felony under the laws of the United States, the State of California, or any other state, government or country, who purchases, transfers, owns, or has in his possession or under his custody or control any handgun or who furnishes any handgun to such person, is guilty of a felony punishable by imprisonment in the state prison for not less than one nor more than five years.

This subdivision (a) shall not apply to a person who has been convicted of a felony under the laws of the United States or any other state, government or country under California law can result in imprisonment of felony punishment or

Any person who furnishes a handgun to a minor is guilty of a felony punishable by imprisonment in the state prison for not more than one year and, upon a subsequent conviction by imprisonment in the state prison for not less than two nor more than five years.

(b) No minor shall be eligible for any license issued pursuant to this chapter.

12067. Upon approval of an application, the Department of Justice shall issue a
license to the television or motion picture production company. The license shall
be valid for a period of not more than two years from the date of issuance of the
license and shall be renewable for additional periods of not more than two years
of such television or motion picture production company.

12068. A television or motion picture production company shall authorize one
representative for motion picture production. The authorization of any
purchasing representative shall terminate if that individual is no longer a member or
employee of the television or motion picture production company or by notice given
to the Department of Justice in a manner specified by regulation promulgated by the
Attorney General.

12070. In addition to any other penalties provided by law, the Department of
Justice shall revoke the license of a television or motion picture production company
for the willful violation of any provisions of this article or any regulation promulgated
thereunder.

12071. The Department of Justice may require persons filing an application pursuant to this article to pay at the time of filing an application, a fee not to exceed
the cost of processing such application.

12072. Each television or motion picture production company shall file a report,
in a manner and at a time specified by the department, that describes, of each transaction or occurrence in which a person is killed or injured as a result of the firing
of a handgun used by such company pursuant to this chapter within ten days of
such incident. The initial report shall be public record open to inspection by any person.

The Attorney General shall annually review and evaluate such reports to ascertain
whether television and motion picture production companies are utilizing handguns
in a manner consistent with public safety. If, after such review, the Attorney General
determines that a television or motion picture production company is not utilizing
handguns in a manner consistent with the public safety, he shall revoke such company's license issued pursuant to this article.

Article 5. Monetary Institutions

12080. It is the intent of the people to permit certain duly licensed institutions to
use handguns for the protection of money, security, treasure, and the transportation of
money. It is the intent of this article to impose strict requirements to protect the public
safety. For the purposes of this article, "department" means the Director of the
Department of Consumer Affairs and "director" means the Director of the Department of
Consumer Affairs.

12081. The department, upon proper application, shall issue or renew a license
to purchase handguns to any monetary institution which complies with the requirement
set forth in Section 12082.

12082. The director shall promulgate regulations to do the following:
(a) To provide for adequate security against theft, misappropriation or misuse
of any handgun owned or a monetary institution or possessed by its authorized
employees.
(b) To provide that employees of the monetary institution who will purchase
handguns are trained in the proper use and safety of handguns. For the
purposes of this subdivision, the course of training prescribed by the department
shall be the minimum standards of training required by this subdivision. The director
shall promulgate a training course which meets those described in this
subdivision.
(c) To provide for adequate insurance against damage or injury caused by any
handgun owned or possessed by any monetary institution or any employee thereof,
including a requirement that each monetary institution maintain insurance against
such damage or injury in such amounts as the director shall prescribe.
(d) To prescribe the maximum number of handguns that may be owned by each
monetary institution, bearing a reasonable relationship to the number of guards
employed by the institution and the geographic area served by the institution.
(e) To assure that handguns owned or possessed pursuant to this article are
used in a manner consistent with the public safety.
(f) Such other regulations as the director deems to be in the interest of public
safety.

12083. Any monetary institution may apply to the department for a license to
purchase handguns for the purpose of guarding money, treasure, bullion, stock
certificates, bonds, or other negotiable instruments.

12084. The director shall promulgate regulations for the form and content of
the application for a license to purchase handguns, including the name, address, and location
of the principal place of business of the monetary institution, and the name, signature,
address, photograph, and fingerprints of an authorized purchasing representative of
such monetary institution, together with such additional information as the director

(d) To assure that handguns possessed pursuant to this article are used in a
manner consistent with the public safety.
(e) To assure the security and safe transportation and storage of the handguns
owned or possessed pursuant to this article.
(f) To proscribe the maximum number of handguns that may be owned by each
security guard company, bearing a reasonable relationship to the number of
watchmen, guards, and patrolmen employed by such security guard company
who are entitled to possess handguns.
(g) To assure that the requirements of this article are complied with.
(h) Such other regulations as the director deems to be in the interest of public
safety.

12090. No person shall be issued or held a license to purchase under this
article unless that person complies with all of the following requirements:
(i) Has a net worth determined by generally accepted accounting principles of
not less than fifty thousand dollars ($50,000.00).
(j) Is a resident of the State of California.
(k) Is under the control and supervision of a manager whose business location
and address are at the principal place of the security guard company.
(l) Shall not furnish uniformed watchmen, guards, or patrolmen to protect
persons or property except pursuant to a written contract in such form as the
director shall prescribe.
(e) Does not derive more than ten percent (10%) of its gross income from
services other than as a security guard (individually or in the aggregate) of
such security guard company. For purposes of this subdivision, the term "affiliate"
means a person that directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries,
controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with the security guard
company. For purposes of this subdivision, the term "associate" means (1) any
corporation or organization (other than the security guard company or a majority
owned subsidiary thereof) in which the security guard company or any officer,
director, partner or other person holding an ownership interest in the security guard
company or any officer, director or partner thereof, is an officer or partner or
directly or indirectly, the beneficial owner of five percent (5%) or more of any
class of equity securities, (2) any relative or spouse of any shareholder, director,
officer, partner or other person holding an ownership interest in the security guard
company, or any relative or spouse of any officer, director, partner or other
director, partner or other person holding an ownership interest in the security guard
company. For purposes of this subdivision, the term "control" (including the
possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the
management and policies of the security guard company, whether through the
ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise). For purposes of this
subdivision, the term "security" means any checks or similar security or
other code.
(f) Does not have any manager, director, officer, employee, member, or partner,
who is under 18 years of age or who is disqualified in Section 12053 or 12053
.5 or who is prohibited by law from possessing a firearm or who is having in
or in his possession or under his custody or control a handgun.
(g) Does not have an aggregate of more than five percent (5%) other ownership
ownership interest owned by individuals who are under 18 years of age or who
are disqualified in Section 12053 or 12053.5 or by any other provision of law
from possessing a firearm or who is prohibited by law from having in or their
custody or control a handgun.

12090.6. Any person who complies with the requirements of this article may
apply to the department for a license to purchase handguns for the purpose of
providing protection of person or property.

12090.8. The department shall provide an application form and such content of
such applications. Such applications shall include all of the following:
(a) The name and mailing address of the security guard company;
(b) The location of the principal place of business of the security guard
company and of all premises from which the security guard company will provide
to be in the interest of public
(c) The name, signature and address of each watchman, guard, or patrolman
to be employed by the security guard company and other identification as
the director may require.
(d) The name, signature, address and photograph of the manager of the
security guard company, and such additional information as the director by
appropriate regulations shall require and to assist in ascertaining and such application to insure
that such manager will abide all written rules and regulations as the security guard company
is hereby required to promulgate regulations thereunder as to the security guard
pursuant thereto.
(e) The necessary information and documentation to show compliance with
the requirements of Section 12090 or other provision of law as to this article.
(f) A declaration under penalty of perjury that the matters stated in the
application are true and correct by the manager of the security guard company.
(g) The name, signature, address, photograph and fingerprints of an authorized
purchasing representative of such security guard company.
(h) Such other information as the director may deem to be in the interest of
public safety.

[Footer text appears to be upside down and illegible]

· (d) The Attorney General shall promulgate regulations and registration forms appropriate to the sale or other transfer of handguns for each of the categories of this section.

(e) Commencing for each handgun thereafter, the information for each handgun shall be maintained in such phases and in such manner as the Attorney General shall by regulation prescribe. A violation of the regulations promulgated by the Attorney General pursuant to this subdivision shall be a misdemeanor, punishable by imprisonment in the county jail for not more than one year or by a fine not to exceed five hundred dollars.

(f) A violation of any of the provisions of this section or regulations promulgated pursuant hereto by any person holding a license provided by law, be grounds for the revocation of such license.

12144. The Attorney General shall promulgate regulations to establish antique record keeping methods to enable identification of each licensed person to whom a handgun has been issued by each manufactory, security guard company, and motion picture production company, together with the serial number of the specific handgun issued to each licensed person, and to enable identification of each antique handgun collector, together with the custodary or control of such antique handgun collector. In addition thereto, such regulations shall also provide for a validation of these regulations shall be grounds for the revocation of any license issued pursuant to this chapter.

12145. Every handgun dealer shall keep a register in which shall be entered the name and address of every person to whom a handgun is sold or transfer, the information specified in Section 12132 for identification of each handgun sold or transferred, and such additional information as the Attorney General shall prescribe. The Attorney General shall promulgate regulations concerning such register.

Such register shall be open to inspection of any peace officer or any regulations promulgated pursuant thereto, shall, in addition to any other penalties provided by law, be grounds for the revocation of the license of such person.

Article 16. Surrender of Handguns to Non-Governmental Organizations

12148.1. In lieu of any transfer of a handgun pursuant to Article 10 (commencing with Section 12140) of this chapter, any person may, beginning on March 1, 1977, and not later than November 30, 1977, sell such handgun pursuant to the provisions of this article, to an person described in subdivision (a), (b), (f), or (g) of Section 12047, or any other organization approved by the Attorney General.

12148.2. Such sale shall be documented by a registration form, on forms prescribed by the Attorney General, which shall include the male, model, serial number, and other information required to identify the handgun being transferred ownership of such handgun to the transferee, signed by the transferor, the name and address of the transferor, the name, address, and purchase license number of handgun dealer's license number of the transferee, the fingerprints of the transferee, such information as the Attorney General shall in regulation prescribe regarding such institution, television or motion picture production company, or security guard company to which transfer, and such additional information as the Attorney General shall in regulation prescribe. The certified copy of the identification of the handgun shall be taken from the handgun and entered in the sheriff of any county or the chief of police of the city where the handgun is located by regulation may prescribe of the completed registration form shall be forwarded by such sheriff or such chief of police department to the Department of Justice, which shall retain such records, and the Department shall verify the identity of the transferee and the validity of the purchase license of handgun dealer's license, shall stamp in validation upon the completed registration form, shall return a validated copy to such sheriff or such chief of police department, shall return such form to the appropriate organization to which the handgun has not been transferred by December 31, 1977, such handgun shall be surrendered to such custodian as the Attorney General shall prescribe, until receipt of such form by such transferee.

(b) No handgun shall be transferred to a transferee pursuant to this article after December 31, 1977.

12148.4. No payment shall be made pursuant to Article 10 (commencing with Section 12140) of this chapter, for any handgun transferred pursuant to this article.

12148.5. The Attorney General shall prescribe such additional regulations to implement this article as he shall deem appropriate.

12150. The Attorney General shall issue appropriate regulations to promote the secure storage and transportation of firearms and ammunition in such quantities as the Attorney General shall by regulation specify, in the possession, custody, or control of the State of California any public or private organization, or any employee thereof.

A violation of any regulation promulgated pursuant to this section shall be punishable by imprisonment in the county jail for a term not exceeding one year or by a fine not exceeding five hundred dollars ($500), or both.

Article 12. Miscellaneous Provisions

12160. No license issued or renewed any license under this chapter, or for the purpose of causing a handgun to be transferred or possessed in violation of the provisions of this chapter, knowingly mistakes of fails to supply any information required to be supplied by this chapter or by any regulation issued pursuant thereto is guilty of a felony punishable by imprisonment in the state prison for not less than one day, and upon a second or subsequent violation of this section is guilty of a felony punishable by imprisonment in the state prison for not less than one year.

(b) Any person who uses any fraudulent or deceptive means for the purpose of causing to be issued, omitted, or renewal any license under this chapter, or for the purpose of causing a handgun to be transferred or possessed in violation of the provisions of this chapter is guilty of a felony and is punishable as provided 12161. It is the intention of this measure to preempt the field of regulation of handguns. No city, county, or city and county shall have the power to enact any ordinance in conflict with this chapter.

12162. The Department of Transportation shall post at all highways crossing state borders, pursuant to regulations promulgated by the Attorney General, at all airports receiving flights from outside California and

sections.conclusion: Although the Commission has not yet adopted the regulation, it was the members' consensus that expenditures for communications which do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or measure are not reportable by groups such as Women For. Therefore you do not have to observe the mass mailing requirements nor report the costs of printing and mailing the calendars.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Women For, a political committee, must report expenditures for printing and mailing calendars of events and comply with mass mailing requirements under the Fair Political Practices Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Women For does not have to report expenditures for printing and mailing calendars or comply with mass mailing requirements, as these communications do not expressly advocate for or against a clearly identified candidate or measure.

The formal advice letter section (pages 1-2) is clearly extracted. The appended materials appear to be context or example documentation but are not part of the actual FPPC response. embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Women For, a political committee, must report expenditures for printing and mailing calendars of events and comply with mass mailing requirements under the Fair Political Practices Act.

CONCLUSION: Although the Commission has not yet adopted the regulation, it was the members' consensus that expenditures for communications which do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or measure are not reportable by groups such as Women For. Therefore you do not have to observe the mass mailing requirements nor report the costs of printing and mailing the calendars.
embedding.first_500_words: April 12, 1976 Roslyn Cooperman, Coordinator Women For: 8913 West Olympic Boulevard Beverly Hills, California 90211 Dear Ms. Cooperman: This letter is in response to your inquiry of April 7, 1976 concerning your calendar of events mailing. The Commission considered at its meeting last week a regulation redefining the term "expenditure" in light of the U.S. Supreme Court's decision in Buckley v. Valeo. Although the Commission has not yet adopted the regulation, it was the members' consensus that expenditures for communications which do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or measure are not reportable by groups such as WOMEN FOR. Therefore you do not have to observe the mass mailing requirements nor report the costs of printing and mailing the calendars. I will keep you informed of the Commission's actions regarding the applicability of disclosure requirements to WOMEN FOR:. If you have further questions, please contact our staff again. Sincerely, Nan Hambleton Special Compliance Representative NH/bw women for: continuing volunteer support and action for issues, candidates and legislation of our choice on local, state and national levels. March 11, 1976 Fair Political Practices Commission 1100 K Street Sacramento, California 95814 Dear Sirs: On March 1, 1976, we sent you copies of our newsletter for March- April 1976, indicating we had sent 1,405 pieces to our 1976 paid- up members, with a handgun control petition enclosed, and 1,032 pieces to the 1975 members who have not yet renewed their member- ships, without the petition enclosed. Because of the weight of the petition, the mailing sent to our 1976 paid-up members cost twice as much. Our question relates to the reporting of financial contribution to Proposition 15, the Nuclear Safeguards ballot measure. In the four page newsletter five half-column lines were included reading as follows: "Californians for Nuclear Safeguards, the coalition of organizations sponsoring the Proposition 15 campaign, needs help of all kinds. You can volunteer your time and/or financial support. Write or call 6022 Wilshire Blvd., L.A. 90036 or 935-1223." Will you please advise is how we estimate, and what we should therefore list in our next report to you, the cost estimate of these five half-column lines. Perhaps there's even a question whether this item in our newsletter constitutes a reportable financial contribution to Proposition 15. Thank you very much for your prompt reply to this request for an advisory opinion. Sincerely, ROSLYN COOPERMAN Coordinator RG/mc 8913 West Olympic Boulevard • Beverly Hills • California 90211 • Telephone 657-7411 COORDINATOR Roslyn Cooperman ASSISTANT COORDINATOR Muriel Blankfort STEERING COMMITTEE Susan Adler, Mary Audun, Myrna Spath, Dorothy Anderson, Alice Jacobs, Edith Barrett, Carla Barsky, Julia Barston, Joanne Bermejo, Joan Couzens, Diane Elgin Sada, Neuma Childs, Urna Colen, Francine Diamond, Rose Marie Epstein, Millie Fine, Gita Flaim, Helen Face, Edythe Gamerall, Darcy Gerber, Laura Lee Gold Nadia Goodwin, Dorothy Green, Faye Healy, Muriel Herber, Marjorie Herron, Rose Hirshfield, Terry Hoffman, Grace Jacobs, Lorraine Jacobs, Phyllis Kahler Marilyn Kizziah, Lee Klein, Ann Kotin, Lucille Lasarow, Lucy Lasher, Bernice
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter from April 12, 1976 to Roslyn Cooperman addressing whether Women For must report calendar mailing costs and comply with mass mailing requirements under campaign finance law. The FPPC advised that such expenditures need not be reported as they do not constitute reportable expenditures under the Buckley v. Valeo standard.
